messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Network Analysis for Ecologists – Practical Skills for Ecological Research
messages.subject_clean: Network Analysis for Ecologists – Practical Skills for Ecological Research
messages.body_plain:
Network Analysis for Ecologists ? Practical Skills for Ecological Research

Learn how to apply network science to ecological data in this focused,
on-demand course from PR Stats. Network Analysis for Ecologists is a 6-hour
course designed to give ecologists the conceptual understanding and
practical tools needed to analyse species interactions and complex
ecological systems using R.

https://prstats.org/course/network-analysis-for-ecologists-nwaepr/

This course is ideal for researchers, postgraduate students, and
practitioners who want to work with food webs, pollination networks,
host?parasite systems, and other ecological networks.

What you will learn


   -

   How to construct and represent ecological networks from interaction data
   -

   Core network metrics and concepts, including degree, centrality,
   modularity, and nestedness
   -

   How to analyse and visualise networks in R using the igraph package
   -

   How to model ecological dynamics using ordinary differential equations
   (ODEs)

Course format and benefits


   -

   Fully on-demand: start anytime and learn at your own pace
   -

   Six hours of recorded content with practical, hands-on examples
   -

   Real ecological datasets and reproducible workflows
   -

   Designed for ecologists with intermediate R experience
   -

   Thirty days of instructor email support
   -

   Lifetime access to course materials
   -

   Digital certificate of completion for professional development


Requirements

Participants need a laptop with R and RStudio installed. No paid software
is required.

Key details


   -

   Duration: 6 hours
   -

   Format: On-demand
   -

   Cost: ?225


If you want to strengthen your ability to analyse ecological networks and
apply these methods directly to your own research, this course provides a
clear, practical introduction grounded in real ecological problems.

Email oliver at prstats.org


Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Generalized Linear Mixed Models (GLMMs) in R  - online course
messages.subject_clean: Generalized Linear Mixed Models (GLMMs) in R  - online course
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
We are excited to announce the 4th edition of our Generalized Linear Mixed Models (GLMMs) in R online course, taking place September 21?24..
 
For more information and to register, please visit:
[ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-work/glmms-in-r ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-work/glmms-in-r )
 
 
This course is designed for graduate students and researchers with experience in generalized linear models who want to deepen their skills to analyze complex grouped and correlated data. You will learn to specify, interpret, and validate GLMMs using the R packages lme4 and glmmTMB, along with hands-on training on model diagnostics and assumption checking using DHARMa.
 
The basics of linear models (LM), generalized linear models (GLM), and ANOVA are reviewed at the start of the course. However, if you do not have a solid understanding of these topics, you may benefit more from our course ?Generalized Linear Models as a Unified Framework for Data Analysis in R,? also offered by Physalia: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/glm-in-r-1/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/glm-in-r-1/ )

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Generalized Linear Mixed Models - Choose from one of 4 on demand courses
messages.subject_clean: Generalized Linear Mixed Models - Choose from one of 4 on demand courses
messages.body_plain:
*Want to Learn Generalized Linear Mixed Models?*


Build strong, practical skills in *mixed effects modelling in R*?from core
GLMM concepts to advanced Bayesian hierarchical models. Our *on-demand
courses* are designed for ecologists, scientists, analysts, and applied
researchers who want rigorous methods with real-world relevance.


*Choose the course that fits your needs:*
------------------------------


*FREE COURSE Introduction to Generalised Linear Mixed Models for Ecologists
<https://prstats.org/course/free-course-introduction-to-generalised-linear-mixed-models-for-ecologists-fmmepr/>*

A concise online introduction to Generalised Linear Mixed Models (GLMMs) in
R, tailored for ecologists and applied scientists.

   - Understand fixed and random effects
   - Fit and interpret GLMMs
   - Learn mixed effects modelling in under 4 hours

------------------------------


*Introduction to Mixed Models
<https://prstats.org/course/introduction-to-mixed-models-immrpr/>*

A practical, on-demand introduction to *linear and generalised mixed models
in R*.

   - Multilevel modelling
   - Nested and crossed designs
   - Random effects
   - Introduction to Bayesian approaches

------------------------------


*Bayesian Approaches to Regression and Mixed Effects Models Using brms
<https://prstats.org/course/bayesian-approaches-to-regression-and-mixed-effects-models-using-brms-barmpr/>*

Learn how to fit *Bayesian regression and mixed models in R* using
the brms package.

   - Modern Bayesian workflows
   - Flexible model specification
   - Applied examples for real data

------------------------------


*Introduction to Bayesian Hierarchical Modelling
<https://prstats.org/course/introduction-to-bayesian-hierarchical-modelling-ibhmpr/>*

A comprehensive *24-hour on-demand course* using R, JAGS, and Stan.

   - Bayesian hierarchical models
   - Analysis of complex structured data
   - Ideal for scientists and data analysts

------------------------------

*Learn at your own pace. Apply methods with confidence. Build better
models.*



Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Want to Learn Movement Ecology and the Analysis of Movement Data?
messages.subject_clean: Want to Learn Movement Ecology and the Analysis of Movement Data?
messages.body_plain:
Want to Learn Movement Ecology and the Analysis of Movement Data?

Animal tracking data are now central to ecological research, conservation,
and wildlife management. This *live online course* provides a thorough,
applied introduction to the *analysis of animal movement data in R*,
combining ecological theory with hands-on statistical workflows.

You will learn how to move from raw tracking data to robust ecological
inference using widely adopted spatial and movement-modelling approaches.
The course is designed to help you analyse complex movement datasets with
confidence and methodological rigour.
------------------------------
Movement Ecology (the Analysis of Movement Data)
<https://prstats.org/course/movement-ecology-the-analysis-of-movement-data-move09/>

Learn to analyse animal movement data using spatial methods, home range
estimation, interaction metrics and resource or step selection models
through hands-on training in R.

This course focuses on practical methods for analysing animal movement
data, with an emphasis on *reproducible workflows in R* and clear
interpretation of results.

*Key topics include:*

   -

   Preparing and exploring animal tracking and telemetry data
   -

   Spatial methods for analysing movement patterns
   -

   Home range estimation and utilisation distributions
   -

   Interaction, overlap, and proximity metrics
   -

   Resource selection functions (RSFs)
   -

   Step selection functions (SSFs) and movement-based models

*What you will gain:*

   -

   A solid understanding of the conceptual foundations of movement ecology
   -

   Hands-on experience applying methods to real movement datasets
   -

   The ability to choose appropriate analytical approaches for different
   ecological questions
   -

   Skills directly applicable to research, conservation, and wildlife
   management contexts

*Who this course is for:*

   -

   Ecologists and wildlife researchers
   -

   Conservation practitioners and analysts
   -

   Graduate students and early-career scientists working with movement data
   -

   Anyone seeking a structured introduction to movement analysis in R

------------------------------

*Learn how animals move, interact with their environment, and use
space?using methods grounded in modern movement ecology.*

Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

Learn how to build robust, defensible species distribution and ecological
niche models in our live online training course: *Standard Modelling
Procedure for Species Distribution and Ecological Niche Modelling (SDMS01)*.

https://prstats.org/course/standard-modelling-procedure-for-species-distribution-and-ecological-niche-modelling-sdms01/
Standard modelling procedure for Species Distribution and Ecological Niche
Modelling (SDMS01)

Species Distribution and Ecological Niche Modelling is an applied R course
teaching standard workflows for building, evaluating, and interpreting SDMs.

This two-day course provides a clear, step-by-step framework for applying
best-practice modelling workflows using R. It is designed for anyone
working with biodiversity, ecology, conservation, or environmental spatial
data who wants practical, reproducible modelling skills.

The course covers:

   -

   Core concepts underpinning species distribution and ecological niche
   modelling
   -

   Preparing and managing species occurrence and environmental data
   -

   Model calibration, evaluation, and interpretation
   -

   Common pitfalls and how to avoid them
   -

   Practical implementation of standardised modelling procedures in R

The training is delivered live online, with recordings available after the
course. All participants receive course materials, example datasets, and
post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 23?24 April 2026
Format: Live online
Duration: 2 days
Fee: ?275

This course is suitable for postgraduate students, researchers,
consultants, and professionals seeking a structured and applied
introduction to SDMs and ENMs.

Full details and registration are available here:

messages.subject_clean: Stable Isotope Mixing Models Using SIBER, SIAR & MixSIAR (SIMM12)
messages.body_plain:
Develop practical skills in stable isotope mixing model analysis with our
live online training course *Stable Isotope Mixing Models Using SIBER, SIAR
& MixSIAR (SIMM12)*.

https://prstats.org/course/stable-isotope-mixing-models-using-siber-siar-mixsiar-simm12/
Stable Isotope Mixing Models Using SIBER, SIAR, MixSIAR (SIMM12)

Stable Isotope Mixing Models in R is an applied course teaching ecologists
how to use SIBER, SIAR, and MixSIAR for dietary analysis.

This hands-on course introduces the theory and implementation of mixing
models for ecological and biogeochemical data. Participants will learn how
to use widely used R packages ? including *SIBER*, *SIAR*, and *MixSIAR* ?
to quantify the contributions of multiple sources to consumer isotopic
signatures, account for uncertainty, and interpret results in an applied
context.

Instructors - Prof Andrew Parnell and Prof Andrew Jackson

Key topics include:

   -

   Fundamentals of stable isotope ecology and mixing model principles
   -

   Preparing and structuring isotope datasets for analysis
   -

   Running and comparing SIBER, SIAR, and MixSIAR models
   -

   Assessing model assumptions, diagnostics, and uncertainty
   -

   Interpreting outputs for ecological and environmental applications

The course is delivered live online, with recordings available after the
event. All participants receive code, example data, and access to
post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 14?15 May 2026
Format: Live online
Duration: 2 days
Fee: ?275

This course is ideal for researchers, postgraduate students, and
professionals in ecology, conservation, fisheries science, biogeochemistry,
and related fields who want to apply mixing models with confidence and
clarity.

Full details and registration are available here:
https://prstats.org/course/stable-isotope-mixing-models-using-siber-siar-mixsiar-simm12/
Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions

Multivariate Analysis of Ecological Communities Using VEGAN (VGNR09)

Analyse ecological community data in R using VEGAN. Learn ordination,
clustering, and multivariate statistics with real datasets.

This practical training provides a structured introduction to multivariate
statistical methods used to explore, visualise, and interpret ecological
community data. Using the R package *vegan*, participants will learn
workflows for analysing species assemblages, environmental gradients, and
ecological patterns commonly encountered in community ecology and
biodiversity studies.

The course focuses on real data and reproducible R workflows, giving you
the tools to:

   -

   Explore and visualise multivariate ecological datasets
   -

   Apply ordination techniques such as PCA, NMDS, and CA/RDA
   -

   Conduct clustering and beta-diversity analyses
   -

   Perform hypothesis testing with PERMANOVA and related methods
   -

   Interpret multivariate outputs in ecological contexts

Delivered live online with recordings available, this course includes code,
datasets, and post-course support for participants.

*Course details*

Format: Live online
Duration: 5 days
Fee: ?475

This training is suitable for postgraduate students, researchers, and

community data analysis in R.

messages.subject_clean: GLMM and time series courses
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,

We have three statistics courses scheduled for 2026. Together, they form 
a natural progression from regression modelling to GLMMs and finally to 
Bayesian time series analysis. You are very welcome to attend one 
course, or combine them depending on your background and interests.

*1. Analysing Aquaculture Data in R*

  * Hybrid course (online for non-IMR staff).
  * 4?8 May 2026.
  * 08.30-15.30 Norwegian time.
  * Price: ?400.
  * This is a five-day course (approximately 40 hours) covering
    introduction to R, data exploration, linear regression, generalised
    linear models, linear mixed-effects models, GLMMs for continuous,
    binary and proportional data, and power analysis.


*2. Introduction to Linear Mixed-Effects Models and GLMM with R*

  * Online live course.
  * 21, 22, 25, 26, 27 May 2026.
  * 14:00?20:00 (UK time).
  * Price: ?500.
  * This course focuses entirely on mixed models and GLMMs. Topics
    include random intercept models, Poisson and negative binomial
    GLMMs, Tweedie and Gamma models, Bernoulli, binomial and beta GLMMs,
    overdispersion, model validation with DHARMa, and power analysis
    (bonus on-demand material).
  * The course contains approximately 15 hands-on exercises using real
    ecological and fisheries datasets.


*3. Bayesian Time Series Analysis ? GAMs, random walks and hierarchical 
time series in inlabru
*

  * Online live course
  * 7?11 September 2026
  * 14:00?20:00 (UK time)
  * Price: ?700
  * This is an applied but advanced course focusing on Bayesian
    regression and mixed models in inlabru, generalised additive models,
    RW2 random walk models, univariate and multivariate time series,
    hierarchical time series, and a wide range of likelihoods for count,
    binary, proportional and zero/one-inflated data.?There is a strong
    emphasis on practical implementation in R, interpretation of
    results, posterior simulation, and model validation.
  * This course assumes prior experience with regression and GLMMs.


All courses include:

  * A one-hour face-to-face video consultation.
  * Access to the course website for 12 months.
  * Annotated R scripts and real datasets.
  * Selected on-demand video material.

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Take your species-distribution modelling skills into the Bayesian realm!
messages.subject_clean: Take your species-distribution modelling skills into the Bayesian realm!
messages.body_plain:
*Take your species-distribution modelling skills into the Bayesian realm!*

*Species Distribution Modelling With Bayesian Statistics (SDMB08)*

https://prstats.org/course/species-distribution-modelling-with-bayesian-statistics-sdmb08/

Join our intensive *three-day live online workshop*: *Species Distribution
Modelling With Bayesian Statistics (SDMB08)*. If you have working knowledge
of species distribution modelling and R and want to deepen your ability to
handle uncertainty, interpret predictions and apply advanced Bayesian
workflows, this course is for you.

*What you will learn*

   -

   How to apply the Bayesian method Bayesian Additive Regression Trees
   (BART) in R for species distribution models, exploiting its strengths in
   prediction accuracy and uncertainty surfaces.
   -

   How to conduct full Bayesian workflows: data preparation, variable/
   predictor selection, model fitting, mapping credible intervals and local
   prediction uncertainty.
   -

   How to handle model interpretation and diagnostics in a Bayesian
   context: posterior summaries, credible intervals, uncertainty surfaces and
   model robustness.
   -

   Hands-on R coding sessions including annotated scripts you can adapt for
   your own species of interest.

*Who should attend*

   -

   Researchers, ecologists, conservation scientists, and spatial-modelling
   practitioners with previous experience in species distribution modelling
   and R.
   -

   Anyone who wants to move beyond standard SDM frameworks into Bayesian
   methods that provide richer inference, improved uncertainty estimation, and
   more robust predictive performance.
   -

   Practitioners working in ecology, biogeography, conservation planning or
   any applied field that requires spatial predictions with quantified
   uncertainty.

*Format & logistics*

   -

   Live online format ? you can participate from anywhere with internet
   access.
   -

   Three full days of training, each day with four hours of interactive
   instruction and practical exercises.
   -

   Sessions will be recorded and available afterward ? making it easier to
   follow up or catch up across time-zones.

*Why this course stands out*

   -

   It introduces the state-of-the-art Bayesian modelling technique BART,
   which recent research shows provides strong performance in species
   distribution modelling.
   -

   Focus on uncertainty quantification: rather than a point-prediction only
   approach, the course equips you to map credible intervals and interpret
   confidence in predictions.
   -

   Hands-on, applicable: you leave the course with code and workflows that
   you can adapt directly to your species modelling projects.

*How to register / next steps*
Visit the PR Stats website for full details, upcoming dates and
registration. Early registration is recommended as places are limited. If
you have any questions about suitability, schedule, or pre required
experience, email oliver at prstats.org with any questions.
https://prstats.org/course/species-distribution-modelling-with-bayesian-statistics-sdmb08/

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Fwd: Advice on spatial scale mismatch: Landscape covariates for 10x10km grid vs averaged point-level buffers
messages.subject_clean: Advice on spatial scale mismatch: Landscape covariates for 10x10km grid vs averaged point-level buffers
messages.body_plain:
I am a PhD student currently working on modeling the spatial distribution
of several bird species in Morocco using opportunistic presence/absence
data (eBird checklists). I am preparing to run Generalized Linear Mixed
Models (GLMMs) using packages like lme4 or glmmTMB, and I am facing a
methodological dilemma regarding the extraction scale of my environmental
and landscape variables.

*The Data:*

   -

   My study area is divided into a 10x10 km grid.
   -

   I have calculated the probability of occurrence (presence/absence) at
   the scale of these 100 km? cells.
   -

   The observation points (checklists) are often spatially biased
   (clustered near roads, villages, etc.).

*The Dilemma:* To extract my landscape covariates (e.g., land cover
percentages, NDVI) to include as fixed effects in my GLMM, I am hesitating
between two approaches:

   1.

   *Whole-cell extraction:* Calculating the landscape variables across the
   entire extent of the 10x10 km cell to match the spatial unit of the
   response variable.
   2.

   *Averaged point-buffers:* Creating a buffer (e.g., 1 km radius) around
   each specific checklist point within the cell, extracting the landscape
   data for these buffers, and then averaging these values to assign a
single
   mean value to the 10x10 km cell.

My current understanding is that approach #2 might introduce a spatial
scale mismatch and suffer from observer bias (modeling the "landscape of
the observer" rather than the available habitat in the cell). Therefore,
approach #1 seems statistically and ecologically more robust for a response
variable modeled at the cell level.

Could anyone share their experience or confirm if approach #1 is the
standard best practice in this context? Any recommended literature
specifically addressing this scale matching in GLMMs using opportunistic
data would also be highly appreciated.

Thank you very much for your time and insights.

Best regards,

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Fwd: Advice on spatial scale mismatch: Landscape covariates for 10x10km grid vs averaged point-level buffers
messages.subject_clean: Advice on spatial scale mismatch: Landscape covariates for 10x10km grid vs averaged point-level buffers
messages.body_plain:
Dear Mohamed,


When dealing with spatial scale mismatch (e.g., 10?10 km landscape
covariates vs. averaged point-level buffers), one practical strategy is to
harmonize predictors and responses onto a common spatial grid before
modeling.


You might find the following package useful:


https://github.com/ManuelSpinola/h3sdm


It supports spatial distribution modeling using H3 hexagonal grids and
allows fitting different modeling algorithms (including GLMs and others) on
a consistent spatial framework. This can help reduce scale inconsistencies
by standardizing the spatial resolution prior to model fitting.


Best regards,

Manuel

Hello R-SIG-ECOLOGY members

Last chance to join our 2-day seminar Multilevel Modeling in R livestreaming February 26?27 with Scott Colwell (University of Guelph and Enablytics). This seminar on Multilevel Modeling in R offers a robust framework for analyzing hierarchical or clustered data, a common structure in ecological and environmental studies. Participants will learn to implement random intercepts and slopes, cross-level interactions, and multilevel logistic regression using R, enabling more nuanced analyses of varying effects across ecological scales or for binary outcomes like species presence. The practical focus on model diagnostics, power analysis for clustered designs, and reproducible R workflows will empower researchers to strengthen the rigor, interpretability, and publishability of their quantitative ecological findings.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/multilevel-modeling-in-r) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur

messages.body_plain:
Single-Cell RNA-Seq Analysis

Live online training covering all stages of single-cell transcriptomic data
analysis ? from experimental design to data QC, normalization, clustering,
differential expression, and biological interpretation.

https://prstats.org/course/single-cell-rna-seq-analysis-scrn02/

Join our four-day live online workshop: Single-Cell RNA-Seq Analysis
(SCRN02). If you?re working with single-cell transcriptome data or planning
to dive into single-cell workflows, this course will guide you from raw
data to interpretable biological insight.

What you will learn

How to design a robust single-cell RNA-Seq experiment, including strategies
for cell capture, sequencing depth and batch control. How to perform
quality control and filtering of single-cell data to ensure high-quality
downstream analysis. How to normalize and process data, cluster cells, and
identify cell types or states. How to conduct differential expression
analysis, trajectory inference or other advanced single-cell analyses. How
to interpret results biologically ? linking clusters, cell types or
trajectories to meaningful insights.

Who should attend

Researchers, postgraduate students and industry professionals working with
single-cell RNA-Seq data. Anyone with basic experience in R, data analysis,
and transcriptomics who wants to build confidence in single-cell workflows.
Those who want to confidently move from raw single-cell data to actionable
biological conclusions.

Course format

Four days of live online sessions (approximately 3? hours each day) in a UK
/ Western European time zone. Interactive lectures, hands-on practical
sessions and discussion time. Course materials, code and example datasets
will be provided ? participants are encouraged to bring their own data for
discussion when possible. Recordings available after each day to support
participants in different time zones.

Why this course stands out

It offers an end-to-end workflow specifically tailored for single-cell data
? from design through QC, clustering, differential expression and
biological interpretation. It emphasises not just how to run analysis
pipelines, but how to understand assumptions, interpret results, and avoid
common pitfalls in single-cell data analysis. Developed and delivered by
experienced bioinformaticians, the course balances theoretical foundations
with applied, hands-on training in a live-online format.

How to register / next steps

Visit the PR Stats website for full course details, upcoming dates and
registration information. Early registration is recommended, as places are
limited. https://prstats.org/course/single-cell-rna-seq-analysis-scrn02/

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Machine Learning for Ecological Time Series
messages.subject_clean: Machine Learning for Ecological Time Series
messages.body_plain:
Learn how to apply modern machine learning methods to ecological
time-series data in our live online course *Machine Learning for Ecological
Time Series (METR01)*.

https://prstats.org/course/machine-learning-for-ecological-time-series-metr01/

This applied R training is designed for ecologists and environmental
scientists who want practical skills for analysing, modelling, and
forecasting ecological time-series data using reproducible workflows.

Participants will work through hands-on examples to understand how machine
learning approaches can be used to detect patterns, make predictions, and
interpret complex ecological dynamics.

The course covers:

   -

   Preparing and structuring ecological time-series data
   -

   Supervised and unsupervised machine learning approaches
   -

   Model training, validation, and performance assessment
   -

   Forecasting ecological trends and dynamics
   -

   Interpreting machine learning outputs in ecological contexts

The training is delivered online with recordings available after the
course, and participants receive course materials, datasets, and
post-course support.

*Course details*
Dates: 13?17 April 2026
Duration: 5 days, approximately 7 hours per day
Format: Recorded sessions with live Q&A
Fee: ?450

This course is suitable for postgraduate students, researchers,
consultants, and professionals working with ecological monitoring data,
population time series, sensor data, or environmental datasets who want to
apply machine learning techniques in R.

Full details and registration:
https://prstats.org/course/machine-learning-for-ecological-time-series-metr01/
Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions
-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Analysing Ecological Data with Detection Error
messages.subject_clean: Analysing Ecological Data with Detection Error
messages.body_plain:
Learn how to analyse ecological survey data while accounting for imperfect
detection in our live online course *Analysing Ecological Data with
Detection Error (AEDD01)*.

https://prstats.org/course/analysing-ecological-data-with-detection-error-aedd01/

Many ecological datasets include detection bias ? species are present but
not observed, counts are underestimated, or survey methods vary in
detectability. This applied R course provides a practical introduction to
statistical methods that explicitly model detection error, helping you
produce more accurate and defensible ecological inferences.

The course covers:

   -

   Understanding detection probability and observation bias in ecological
   surveys
   -

   Occupancy models and abundance models with imperfect detection
   -

   Survey design considerations and data requirements
   -

   Model fitting, validation, and interpretation in R
   -

   Applying detection-error methods to real ecological datasets

Delivered live online with recordings available, participants receive
course materials, datasets, and post-course support.

*Course details*
Format: Live online
Duration: 2 days
Includes: Course materials, code examples, and post-course support

This course is suitable for postgraduate students, researchers, ecological
consultants, and conservation practitioners working with survey,
monitoring, or citizen-science datasets where detectability is uncertain or
variable.

Full details and registration:
https://prstats.org/course/analysing-ecological-data-with-detection-error-aedd01/
Email oliver at prstats.org with any questions.

-- 
Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] (online course) Time Series Analysis and Forecasting in R - 20-24 July
messages.subject_clean: (online course) Time Series Analysis and Forecasting in R - 20-24 July
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
We are pleased to announce the Physalia online course: Time Series Analysis and Forecasting in R

Dates: 20?24 July 
Schedule: 16:00?19:00 (Berlin time)
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/time-series-in-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/time-series-in-r/ ) 
 
 
This course focuses on modern approaches to time series analysis for ecological and environmental data. While traditional forecasting methods are briefly introduced, the main emphasis is on dynamic Bayesian models that can handle features commonly found in real ecological time series, such as non-linearity, overdispersion, missing data, and temporal dependence.
 
Participants will learn how to:
Explore and visualise time series data
       2. Fit dynamic regression and smoothing models with temporal processes
 
      3. Critically assess, compare, and visualise fitted models
 
       4. Produce forecasts and evaluate their performance using probabilistic approaches
 
 
Best regards,
Carlo
--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D
